data.title: FX Levels For EUR/USD, USD/CAD And GBP/USD: USD Dumps Amid Peace Repricing
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The US dollar may have just seen its brightest days at the cost of a world-shaking US-Iran-Israel conflict. Having bullied through a gigantic rebound since February, the greenback had been invincible. Petrodollar trades and higher-for-longer US rates tend to largely support the global reserve currency.

Add to it historic bearish positioning against it, and traders saw the perfect conditions to push the USD to 11-month highs. This was enough to question whether dedollarization was just a fantasy rather than a proper regime change in financial markets.

Nevertheless, things changed in the past couple of weeks. President Trump, frustrated by developments such as lower stock markets, higher commodity prices, and a more expensive dollar - economic trends he publicly dislikes - found himself in a pessimistic mood right ahead of the November midterm elections.

Hence, the Administration has been pushing aggressively for a truce, specifically because this aligns with the prolonged mid-April deadline for the conflict to end.

While a proper peace deal hasn't yet been reached, diplomatic attempts are, for now, heading the right way, leading to a consequent tumble in the US dollar.

You can see the direct result from today's FX performance. The US dollar lost around 2% of its value against a basket of major currencies and has begun to revert to its trajectory.

Reaching a key support level, however, currency traders could be taking a break from their dollar sales. We will explore key levels right after taking a quick look at the Dollar Index chart.

Stalling at the major 98.00 support, the US dollar could be seeing a short-term pause in its selling; hence, it is important not to get caught in the crosswinds of a potential reversal.

However, this could provide opportunities to catch a pullback in the currency and offer decent setups in major FX pairs!

In preparation for the next phase of a longer-run dollar selloff (conditional on the conflict really coming to an end), we will look at three key FX majors and their intraday timeframes to see how the range in the Dollar Index affects their own currency pairs: EUR/USD, GBP/USD, and USD/CAD.

EUR/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

EUR/USD quickly profited from the truce to rally 2,000 to 1.18. However, meeting a significant resistance zone and overbought RSI conditions, the odds for upside continuation from here are slim (in the short run).

In such conditions, buying on a pullback makes the most sense.

  • Aggressive buyers could look at 1.1750 for entries (less optimal)
  • The best setup would be located at the 1.17 to 1.1720 March pivot
    • This would require the peace process to keep progressing.
  • Aggressive sellers could look to enter at current levels and will want to see a break below 1.17 to add to their positions (in the event of worsening fundamentals)

Levels of interest for EUR/USD trading

Resistance levels

  • Resistance zone around 1.18 (+/- 150 pips)
  • 1.1850-1.1860 resistance
  • Morning highs: 1.18114
  • Sep 2021 highs – resistance 1.19 to 1.1950 Zone

Support levels

  • 1.17 to 1.1720 March pivot
  • Pivotal support 1.1625 - 1.1635
  • 1.1540 to 1.1570 war support
  • War and August 2025 lows: 1.14

USD/CAD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

USD/CAD had taken quite a lead on its reversal from the higher part of its 1.3550 to 1.3950 range. Finding buyers at the 1.3750 pivotal support, the pair has already begun its pullback; hence it could be wise to wait for a retest of the higher bound of the 1.38 pivot zone and bear channel (1.3810) to enter shorts.

Coming back above the 4H 50-period MA (1.3862) would put back the advantage to the bulls.

Levels of interest for USD/CAD trading

Resistance Levels

  • 1.38 pivot +/- 150 pips
  • 1.3850 resistance
  • 1.39 to 1.3925 support turned resistance
  • 1.3950 range highs

Support Levels

  • 1.3750 pivotal support
  • 1.3630 to 1.3660 key support
  • 1.3550 main 2025 support
  • 1.35 key psychological support

GBP/USD 4H Chart and Technical Levels

The pound is under similar conditions as the euro but grabs the upper hand in terms of strength and momentum against the US dollar. GBP/USD is reaching overbought conditions and could see a decent support retest in coming times after extending without pullbacks.

Aggressive buyers will look at the 1.3500 psychological level to catch a wider rally in the pair. More defensive pullback traders will have to wait for a retest of the key pivot, which would only be reached if the tone sours ahead of Thursday's US-Iran talks.

Levels of interest for GBP/USD trading

Resistance levels

  • December resistance 1.36 (testing)
  • Resistance 1.37 zone
  • 2025 resistance around 1.38
  • 1.3850 to 1.39 2021 resistance

Support levels

  • 1.35 minor support
  • Key pivot and support 1.34 to 1.3440
  • Pivotal support 1.3250 - 1.33
  • 1.32 war support

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: Cisco: The AI Top-Line Boom Can't Hide The Free Cash Flow Problem
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) is still paying for the company record acquisition of Splunk. The most recent quarter shows revenue is accelerating, but tripled balance sheet debt and structurally compressed free cash flow generation, which leaves Cisco unable to accelerate further AI investments.

With a payout ratio approaching 60% and annual dividend growth stuck at 2.5%, the income case for Cisco is not keeping up in real terms against inflation. Finally, the current yield of 2.1% sits well below its own 5-year average of 2.83%, meaning the stock is historically expensive relative to its own dividend average.

Splunk Changed the Balance Sheet Permanently

The primary driver of this low dividend growth is the March 2024 Splunk acquisition, the largest deal in the company's history. Cisco funded the $28 billion transaction almost entirely with debt.

Before Splunk, Cisco operated as a net cash company with a healthy free cash flow stream available for dividends and buybacks. After Splunk, the company carries $19.8 billion in net debt and must service $28 billion in total obligations.

Goodwill on the Rise!

As a result of the acquisition, goodwill has increased significantly. Splunk, an AI and data driven corporate security and monitoring software, is by definition asset-light.

Stated equity is up slightly to $46.8 billion compared to the $44 billion before the acquisition; however, $59.1 billion of that now sits in goodwill alone.

Splunk is seemingly a great fit by bridging the gap between parent Cisco's networking hardware and software analytics. However, the entire purchase price entered the balance sheet as debt and goodwill, instantly wiping out $25 billion worth of tangible shareholder equity. The effect is showing today, two years later.

Free Cash Flow is the Real Problem

The debt-financed acquisition is also affecting cash flow generation.

During FY2024, Cisco spent $3 billion more on shareholder returns than it generated in free cash flow. While FY2025 improved, it still came in below 0, indicating the company is funding shareholder returns with debt issuance/savings rather than organic cash generation. Also worth noting operating cash flow still comes in far below the FY2023 prior to Splunk. On a TTM level it is even worse at FCF of -$1.3 billion after shareholder returns.

The Penny-Per-Quarter Machine

This leads to the dividend review. Cisco has raised its dividend for 14 consecutive years.

However, the growth rate is kept alive with an annual 1 cent increase to the quarterly dividend per share, like clockwork. The 5-year CAGR of 2.65% doesn't keep up with inflation over the same window.

In terms of purchasing power, Cisco's dividend has been shrinking, and an income investor buying Cisco at current prices is accepting a payout that loses purchasing power annually.

To be fair, you can't look at the dividend growth rate isolated - you need to take the starting base into consideration as well.

Cisco's starting yield is significantly higher than many high-growth technology peers like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO). For long-term dividend investors, betting on a company with a dividend growth rate not keeping up with inflation is a bad bet waiting to happen.

The AI Narrative Does Not Fix the Dividend Math

Cisco's Q2 FY2026 (ended January 31) delivered $15.35 billion in revenue, up 9.7% year on year, alongside non-GAAP EPS of $1.04 and non-GAAP gross margins of 67.5%. In other words, Cisco's top-line execution is flawless right now.

Management has used the AI infrastructure boom to push quarterly revenue into its fourth consecutive period of growth: $14.15 billion, $14.67 billion, $14.88 billion, $15.35 billion. Management now expects to take over $5 billion in AI orders for the full fiscal year, supported by a newly announced 1 GW infrastructure joint venture with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and HUMAIN.

However, Q2 FY2026 FCF dropped to $1.54 billion. This decline is not driven by heavy capital investment into new AI infrastructure, as CapEx remained flat at roughly $250 million per quarter. Until free cash flow stabilizes consistently above an average $3 billion per quarter, the capital allocation constraints around the dividend will remain.

Management confirmed AI revenue for the full fiscal year is still guided to roughly $3 billion. For AI to meaningfully accelerate dividend growth, the $8.4 billion+ annual order run rate must convert into consistent free cash flow for Cisco to offset the Splunk debt burden. At the current pace, the timeline for AI to play catch-up is measured in fiscal years, not quarters.

Valuation: Historically Expensive Compared to its Own Yield

The current 2.08% dividend yield is well below 2.83%, which means the stock is historically expensive relative to its own average.

As seen in the chart above, you are paying a premium on forward P/E while getting a lower direct return in dividends, and the same story goes when looking at the FCF yield, almost half of the 5-year average.

Looking at competitor valuation gives a more nuanced story. At first glance, Cisco looks relatively cheap on several parameters next to Microsoft and broadly comparable to Broadcom on an EV/EBITDA basis, as well as dividend yield. The points made in this article should give an indication why. Cisco is largely priced as a legacy hardware provider struggling to pivot, wrapped in a high debt.

Microsoft and Broadcom both have higher growth rates, warranting the higher multiples. It's a classic value trap setup for Cisco: it looks cheap, but it's cheap for a reason.

Using Dividend Yield Theory, fair value is derived from normalizing the yield back toward historical averages:

The yield-based model and the P/E model suggest the stock is significantly overextended compared to historical norms. The fair value estimate comes in at $69, derived by splitting the difference between yield and P/E mean reversion. That base implies investors buying at $82 are paying a 19% premium for an AI opportunity yet to materialize while looking into years of 1 penny dividend hikes.

Where I Could Be Wrong on the Bear Case

The AI Orders Convert Faster: Management guided for $3 billion in AI revenue this year, but they announced AI orders for $2.1 billion in Q2 alone. If their backlog conversion rates increase, cash flow will improve much faster than expected, breaking right past the $3 billion quarterly ceiling. This leaves room for an increased debt paydown schedule, leaving more room for reinvestment for future growth

Splunk Margins Actually Work: If Splunk's software meshes cleanly with Cisco's networking hardware, operating margins are expected to expand. Faster implementation will again improve cash flow generation.

Aggressive FED rate Cuts: Lower rates let Cisco refinance debt at a lower rate, freeing up cash due to lower finance costs. Next, investors start chasing yield, driving up share prices, regardless of the underlying business dynamics.

What to Watch for on May 13th

Cisco reports Q3 FY2026 on May 13th. The key metric to watch is whether FCF recovers from the Q2 level ($1.54 billion, depressed by a one-time $2.3 billion transition tax payment) back to the $3-4 billion range required to support the current capital return program without further debt issuance.

Watch for three things on the call:

  • Management commentary on Splunk's cloud transition drag - explicitly flagged as continuing through H2 FY2026
  • Any update on AI order backlog conversion to recognized revenue, and
  • Continuous top-line growth

Conclusion

Cisco is fundamentally well-run with payouts that are highly reliable. However, they are still working out the cost of the Splunk acquisition.

For investors building compounding portfolios, the dividend math simply does not work at current valuation levels. A 2.03% yield growing at 2.5% annually produces $2.03 in Year 1 and $2.53 in Year 10 on a $100 investment.

The Splunk debt functionally restricts capital return flexibility until the balance sheet naturally deleverages, maintaining the penny-per-quarter dividend hike as a firm ceiling.

Rating: Sell, based on fair value estimate: $69

data.title: Vertiv: The $15 Billion Backlog, Liquid Cooling Dominance, And The AI Infrastructure Trade Wall Street Is Still Underpricing
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT) offers the most liquid and operationally mature chances to take advantage of the current physical infrastructure layer of AI development—instead of chips or cloud software, it is the thermal and power systems of the chip that are critical to its performance. Without cooling, a data center running NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) Blackwell or Vera Rubin GPUs at 50–100 kW per rack can overheat. Without a power distribution system, it will not start. Over six decades, Vertiv has built a robust presence in domain expertise, customer relationships, a global service network, and a product offering that has seen it become the partner of choice for every large hyperscaler in building next-generation AI infrastructure. The $15 billion backlog and the 252% Q4 order surge are the financial fingerprint of that structural advantage finally being recognized at scale.

The thesis is grounded in four verifiable facts. First, Q4 2025 organic orders surged 252% year-over-year—the strongest quarter in the company's history—pushing backlog to $15 billion with a book-to-bill ratio of approximately 2.9x, per the Vertiv Q4 2025 8-K filed with the SEC on February 11, 2026. Second, management expects the company to have revenue of $13.25–$13.75 billion and adjusted EPS of $5.97–$6.07 by 2026—both exceeding analyst consensus by more than 10%—and a target operating margin of 25% in 2029. Third, the data center cooling market is forecast to grow at a 22.3% CAGR through 2033 to reach $128 billion, per Grand View Research. Fourth, Vertiv joined the S&P 500 effective March 23, 2026—an institutional ownership catalyst that is still working through index funds positioning across active managers globally.

The valuation of VRT is marked by approximately 46x forward earnings—not common by any conventional industrial standard. The counterargument is very clear: a P/E ratio on 43% EPS growth produces a PEG ratio of approximately 1.07, which is lower than the PEG of the S&P 500 index on current consensus estimates. The market is putting an industrial multiple on a company with software-like order acceleration and hypergrowth backlog dynamics. The correction of that categorization error is the investment return available for Vertiv.

The Orders Surge: Understanding the 252% Signal

The 252% year-over-year surge in Q4 orders is the single most important data point in the VRT thesis—and it is frequently misread. Until 2023 and early 2024, hyperscalers and colocation providers had been tentatively fulfilling orders until the application of AI development had opened up new opportunities. Per the Vertiv Q4 2025 earnings call transcript (Investing.com, February 11, 2026), on February 11, 2026, the CEO of Vertiv, Giordano Albertazzi, praised the company due to its indisputable signs of another great quarter. The book-to-bill ratio of Vertiv in Q4 was 2.9x, with $2.90 coming in new orders per new revenue dollar, meaning that Vertiv has already burned off all of the revenue up to 2027 and 2028.

An even stronger description of the rise in order up to 2025 exists. The increase in the organic orders was about 60% in Q1 2025, +54% in Q2, and +60% in the third quarter. The cumulative backlog more than doubled in a year and resulted in a +252% rise in post-quarter (Q4) recovery. This new band is not a sudden increase in numbers but rather a surge in commitments from hyperscaler customers to enter into agreements at the same time (Q4 2025). Perhaps this is the sole explanation, which is reasonable at the least as compared to what we get to know about data center CapEx announcements nowadays. The requirements of the thermal management infrastructure are already in place, including the five leading hyperscalers in the USA, all of whom will chip in $660-690 billion (CapEx) by 2026. The majority of the orders were done with Vertiv, which is the most popular manufacturer of liquid cooling systems across the globe.

The most probable demonstration of structural competitiveness is the use of liquid cooling. NVIDIA Blackwell architecture requires more specialized air-conditioning approaches than that of fans and computer room air conditioners when the rack densities are over 20 kW per rack. In 2026-2027, NVIDIA is releasing the Vera Rubin platform that can produce racks up to 50-100 kW. These densities can only support CDUs and direct liquid cooling loops. Vertiv OneCore and SmartRun are tailored to AI settings, and they are an established partner of NVIDIA regarding their liquid cooling infrastructure, which will be launched in 2025. This hardware, software, and a network of services worldwide are combined to provide an insurmountable switching cost barrier in sustaining the infrastructure throughout its operational life.

Cooling TAM and Margin Expansion: The Two-Vector Growth Engine

The financial trend that Vertiv will pursue until 2029 is twofold: an expanding TAM of liquid cooling and multi-year growth in operating margins, currently around 20% and targeted by management at 25% by 2029. According to Grand View Research's data center cooling market analysis, the global cooling market will attain a value of $128.31 billion by 2033, with the market increasing by 22.3% compared to that in 2026. Because of the product lines, Vertiv has grown its addressable market revenue by approximately 15–20% with a CAGR of over 22%, and it is projected to be steady up to the end of the decade. The baseline includes no significant revenues of the already established competitors who actively seek the conversion to liquid cooling, or revenues that Vertiv was expecting due to the conversion.

The second half of FY2022 operating margin of Vertiv was around 6.2%. The rate of growth had decreased to approximately 20.2% by FY2025. The target for management is to reach 25% by 2029, with guidance being set at 22.5% by 2026. The growth is largely due to fixed manufacturing costs leveraged with increased revenue, a predicted change in product mix to liquid cooling systems and software with higher unit margins, and productivity gains attributed to lean manufacturing efforts. The supplier must be flexible in pricing to be considered in many hyperscaler programs. The company increased its revenue by 27% and its margin by 20%–22.5%. A premium multiple is an implication of the fact that the EBITDAs are increasing at a higher rate as compared to revenues, which causes an earnings leverage.

Segment Performance and Competitive Position

Vertiv is based in three locations, namely the Americas, APAC, and EMEA, which are huge revenue-generating and growth areas. Per the Vertiv FY2025 Annual Results, Americas revenue increased by 34% in FY2025 to approximately $6.7 billion, largely because of the hyperscaler and artificial intelligence infrastructure popularity in Virginia, Texas, Oregon, and Arizona. The speeding up of European data center investment, which reached about $2.0 billion, was largely due to the EU AI Act compliance deadline and country-specific AI strategy investment. The APAC region's revenue decreased by almost 9.6% year-on-year due to restructuring and regional delays. Hopefully, the reorganization of the management of EMEA will lead to the regaining of profits by 2026, whereas APAC is likely to recover with the development of the regional AI infrastructure on the basis of the first hyperscaler clusters in Singapore, Tokyo, and Sydney.

The liquid cooling environment is not favorable to Vertiv at present, but the present investment is worth bearing considering the good enlistment terms within a period of three to five years. Among the other viable competitors, such as Schneider Electric, Eaton, and numerous minor thermal players, Vertiv is distinguished by its experience of over 60 years of data center experience. Its comprehensive worldwide service and maintenance network is due to liquid cooling failures that may cause complete shutdown of whole server pods, proven NVIDIA alliances, a full line of products that span the entire thermal chain, such as CDUs, in-row coolers, and air-handling units. The qualification time needed by new users of the hyperscaler programs, who must prove reliability at scale, is a matter of years, and not months.

Valuation and Price Target

The valuation of Vertiv requires a growth-adjusted model to compare it with the P/E of an average industrial company. VRT is trading at 46x based on adjusted EPS of $6.02, which is much lower than Eaton and Emerson at 24x and 21x, respectively. Eaton and Emerson have a cyclical growth of about 10% and 8% in their EPS respectively and Vertiv is expected to record a +43% growth in its EPS by 2026. VRT comes up with a PEG of 1.07 by dividing the P/E multiple by the annual growth rate of the EPS, as compared to 1.5–1.7x and 2.4x of S&P 500 and Eaton, respectively. However, despite the nominal P/E being the highest, the earnings of the company still lagged behind the growth rate. As opposed to common consensus, Vertiv is the cheapest alternative on the list. Investors are using the wrong value instrument because they are not willing to pay a 46x P/E of a company that is growing EPS at 43%.

Broadly speaking, the same conclusion has been arrived at by the analyst community. According to StockAnalysis Vertiv forecast data (April 2026), 22 of the 26 analysts covering it have a Buy or Strong Buy rating, but none have a Sell rating. The next-year bear case is $212, assuming a normalized order momentum and a multiple contraction is realized, which will be a result of a large decline in hyperscaler AI CapEx in H2 2026. I expect to achieve a price of $310 based on three factors: (1) a consensus EPS estimate of $6.02 for 2026; (2) a 43% EPS growth rate; and (3) a PEG-implied target multiple of approximately 51.5x. A PEG yield of 1.15x is pegged on an increase of the EPS of 45%, a modest S&P-PEG+ premium, and the multiple target is approximately 51.5x. Assuming that they reach their targeted 25% operating margin by that time, despite the massive decline in VRT by Q4 2025, the stock price would be more than $300+ by 2029 with an EPS of $10+. The $310 target is a modest rise of 11% but it is nevertheless compelling by risk-reward standards against the trend line of estimated earnings, which extends out to 2027–2029.

Risk Factors

Hyperscaler CapEx Deceleration

The slow sales of hyperscaler AI CapEx threaten the VRT thesis because it may lead to a decrease in the volume of orders to the critical point to maintain an organic increase in revenue. The large backlog of $15 billion is a valuable safety net. The revenue for 2026 is $13.5 billion, and it will cover more than a year's worth of revenue, even if there are no new orders to make in the future.

APAC Restructuring and Revenue Recovery

In FY2025, APAC revenue went down by 9.6%, and the region continues to restructure. The management estimates that LT geography will play a smaller role in contributing to the growth of VS in the subregional AI infrastructure. The near term margin headwind in EMEA is low because of the restructuring cost.

Tariff and Supply Chain Exposure

Vertiv is represented in the manufacturing industry in the U.S., Europe, and Asia. Tariff effects, which are already in place, have been incorporated in the operating margin guidance of Q4 2025. Possible Sino-US trade conflicts or the emergence of a new type of electronic part can be signs that margins will be decreasing in the guided ranges. Historically, management has had to raise prices to equalize tariffs, and contracting legislation and the timing of price raising can lead to short-term pressure on the margin with repricing contracts.

Conclusion: The Heat Is the Signal

Every NVIDIA GPU shipped into a hyperscale data center produces heat. That heat must be removed; otherwise, the GPU cannot operate. The absence of an optional upgrade has meant that NVIDIA has had to publish their Blackwell and Rubin architectures with a liquid-cooling-only specification for a while, and the shift to liquid-cooled hardware is a common occurrence. It is a matter of physical necessity. For the past 60 years, Vertiv has been able to dissipate heat with confidence, taking into account thousands of details and having the necessary pipeline and sponsors. The margins have been increasing at the rate at which the demand for that skill is rising.

I anticipate approximately 11% upside to my $310 price target. The more likely scenario for the future is to have a $10+ EPS, a 25% operating margin, and a 22% TAM per annum. The linking of every deployed unit to a service network provides repeated revenue. In my opinion, Vertiv's investment thesis is as simple as a structurally advantaged infrastructure franchise with a PEG of 1.1x throughout the AI infrastructure cycle.

data.title: Accenture: Downgrade To Hold As Near-Term Growth Acceleration Seems Unlikely
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Accenture plc (ACN) was an upgrade to a buy rating because the growth outlook had improved. Booking trends were solid, and GenAI-related demand was becoming a more tangible growth lever. After reviewing the latest developments, I no longer think the setup is as clean. The AI story still looks stronger and more credible, but bookings momentum has slowed, pricing is only mixed, and ACN is relying more on M&A today. I am still bullish in the long term but am now more cautious in the near term.

The AI Story Still Looks Good

There were two important points to my buy rating in January this year. First, bookings had finally improved. Second, AI was starting to be a real growth driver. After looking at the recent developments, I think the second point looks better, but the first one does not (will talk about this in the next section).

For the AI story, there are tangible data points to make the story more credible. Management said ACN now has >1,400 advanced AI clients, with ~100 added in the latest quarter. More importantly, they also said more clients are moving from proof of concept to production, and this matters the most for the story, I believe, because production work implies clients are actually changing workflows and spending real money, and that makes ACN harder to displace. The knock-on impact here is that AI isn’t just driving growth on its own. It is also pulling more work as it gets adopted. In other words, when clients look to adopt an AI solution, they end up adopting other solutions that ACN offers too. We know this because management said at least one in every two advanced AI projects leads to a data project.

Now, this tells me the opportunity is not just ACN helping in AI implementation. It is the entire "whole product." Clients don’t just need the AI solution itself; they need to have the entire infrastructure (data, cloud, security, etc.) to make it work. And since ACN already has expertise and scale in those areas, it is natural that clients lean on ACN for those solutions too.

What Keeps This From Being a Bullish Update

The main reason I cannot stay as positive as I was before is that the bookings signal is no longer as clean. It is good to see that ACN posted record bookings of $22.1 billion and said it had a record 41 clients with quarterly bookings >$100 million, but growth slowed to just 1% in local currency. The dollar booking number is good, but the change in momentum is not. If this forward indicator is slowing, it is hard to put forth the case that demand is accelerating.

ACN could rely on M&A to supplement the slowing bookings growth, but I am not sure if it is going to be that easy. Previously, I was comfortable with ACN relying more on acquisitions (~$3 billion deployment in FY2026), because I viewed that as a reasonable trade-off to enhance its AI capabilities and protect the longer-term growth runway. But at $5 billion (new spending target) and with acquisition valuations rising (my opinion is AI talent and a strong product portfolio are now in heavy demand, and that should drive up valuation), the return on investment is likely to be lower. If ACN is paying more but getting less, the success of the M&A strategy will depend much more on integration execution (realizing synergies) and how quickly these deals can turn into real revenue. The bear case here is that if the acquired assets take longer to scale or if returns come in lower because entry multiples are higher, then ACN could end up taking the near-term margin hit without getting enough growth back quickly enough.

Pricing is another reason I am downgrading to a hold rating. My original view was that more advanced AI work should support better pricing over time because those systems are more deeply embedded in client workflows and are harder to replace. What they said was more limited. Specifically, they said pricing improved in some areas of the business, but the overall environment remains highly competitive. In other words, pricing does not appear to be getting worse, but it is also not improving enough for me to treat it as a real support to my thesis yet.

Valuation

Based on the chart, ACN is now trading at about 13.2x NTM PE versus roughly 20.3x for the S&P index, which puts the relative ratio at ~0.65x, near the low end of the historical range. That does look cheap at face value, but the issue for me is in the current setting, revenue growth does not look attractive enough to support a stronger re-rating case. Consensus is still pointing to only low-to-mid single-digit top-line growth over the coming quarters. That is not the growth profile that will help to close the valuation gap quickly, in my view. In simpler terms, ACN may continue to be cheap for longer than expected as the market is waiting for a reason to drive multiples up (which I think is its revenue growth acceleration). With booking momentum slowing, pricing still mixed, and M&A not being the same magnitude of growth driver, I think the discount is understandable for now.

All in all, my view is that the stock has become more attractive on valuation, but not attractive enough to outweigh the weaker near-term narrative.

Conclusion

My view for ACN is now a hold rating. I still think ACN is well positioned to benefit from rising enterprise AI adoption, and it still remains strong with large clients. But the near-term setup is no longer as attractive as it looked a few months ago. Record bookings are encouraging, but the slowdown in bookings growth, limited pricing improvement, and heavier reliance on M&A make it hard to argue that growth is set to re-accelerate soon.

data.title: Talos Energy: Improved Fundamentals, Still Room To Run
data.description:
Since the last time I talked about Talos Energy (TALO), the business itself improved quite a bit. The company is now generating real cash flow, the execution is better, and the production base appears to be more stable. Since my last article, one thing has been left unchanged: Talos is still very dependent on oil prices. That is obvious, I know.

It was and still is a cyclical player, which can survive oil cycles. The thesis then was pretty simple. The company's balance was clean with 0.7x leverage, together with the Katmai and Sunspear projects, which I predicted would generate real cash flow. At the time, the valuation seemed too low for the potential.

Now that we have new numbers and real execution, it is worth checking the company out again.

What Changed ?

The difference is that we have more clarity now on how the company is managing this risk in the short term. Currently around 47% of 2026 Q1 production is hedged. Structure is quite typical; around 15 thousand barrels per day have a fixed price of $66, and another 14.3 thousand bpd goes through collar-type hedges with around a $59 floor and a $68-69 ceiling.

In one way it means that the downside is limited, but the upside is limited too. For me, this moment is very important, as currently we live in an elevated oil prices environment. And it appears that the company might not be able to fully capture these upward oil swings. On one hand, it is good, that if oil went down to $55 or lower, the company would have a buffer through hedges. However, now when the oil is above $70, part of the upside is simply not calculated into cash flow due to same collars. In long-term period, the situation does not change; when the hedges are over, the company will be able to capture oil prices. So, I am not worried, as the story is the same. Talos tries to soften short-term swings, which is understandable, but in reality, I think that long-term return will depend simply on oil cycles.

This, in my opinion, is the biggest change since I covered the stock. In 2025 production reached 94.6 Mboe/d, which shows to me that the base is stable and there are no unexpected shortfalls. Tarantula platform throughput increased to 38 MBoe/d after debottlenecking. It is an important infrastructure upgrade as one of the bottlenecks disappeared.

About Katmai, I do not want to overdo it with superlatives, but the fact is that this is one of the stronger assets in the portfolio. It is important, as we were talking and expecting; now we see actual proof of that. Execution now can be seen, and the thesis, in fact, is changing from when to how much they can pump.

Daenerys is also becoming another catalyst. Discovery is already confirmed, and it is planned to do appraisal drilling in 2026 Q2. From what I gathered, the management expects 100-300 MMboe gross potential. Just don't get your hopes up; this is a very early stage, and until we get appraisal results, there are more optionality factors than core value. The important part here is that Daenerys is becoming a catalyst, but for now, I would not bet on it.

2026 Guidance

This is what I really wanted and we did not have before. It is clearer 2026 guidance. The company expects around 85-90 Mboe/d production, which, at first sight, is a drop compared to 2025. But let's understand the context. It is mainly connected to planned downtime and natural field decline. I did not notice any structural problems, to be fair. At the same time, CAPEX is guided to be high, around $500-550 million, which again shows that Talos is still a capital-intensive business. Oil cut around 73% also remains high. Overall, the guidance confirms what we already see. The business is stable, but not light and very dependent on cycles.

Financial Health

If we look at the numbers, Talos generates strong cash flow. In 2025, operating cash flow was around $936 million and free cash flow around $418 million. The numbers look very good and strong. I know that some might point out the GAAP loss ($494 million), but it is mainly due to $454 million in non-cash impairments, and a ceiling test adjustment, and it is not operational weakness in my opinion. However, the CAPEX is still around $500 million, which means that the majority of generated cash is going back into the business. It is understandable that the business is capital intensive, and I believe that it restricts how much cash is left for shareholders.

I think that the balance is strong here. The company has $363 million in cash, and overall their position is good in my opinion, with liquidity of $965 million. Debt is around $1.25 billion, and at the current EBITDA level, it gives around 0.7x leverage. Balance itself is not really a risk. I call it a comfortable one for a cyclical business. The leverage seems to be controlled to me.

The management is creating value, which is great. In 2025, $119 million was used for buybacks. It is around 29% of their free cash flow. In a year, they bought around 12.6 million shares, while the outstanding shares count dropped 7%. I believe it is great as it gives more confidence that the management is not only thinking about CAPEX but shareholder returns too. That gives me confidence because it gives some flooring for share price even at higher volatility.

What To Expect?

As I already established, the company is now showing quite good results. We have an earnings report coming on May 5th, where we will see whether the company can maintain that 85-90 MBoe/d guidance. We should keep in mind that Q1 production could be lower. I expect around 84-88 MBoe/d due to planned downtime. I am mentioning this as the results might be scary at first, but it is not a structural problem. Also, it's worth watching how the cash flow looks at current oil prices, but I would not get excited due to the hedges I mentioned. You should be looking at CAPEX discipline as it remains high, and any signal that CAPEX is decreasing could mean more cash for shareholders. As mentioned above, Daenerys appraisal in 2026 Q2 might become another strong catalyst.

Risks

Despite good results, we should still talk about risks. Talos is dependent on oil, so any change in oil prices has an effect on it. Long-term oil price change can change cash flows quickly, and the stock price itself tends to correlate with oil. Hedges that we talked about before are a short-term protection.

We still have execution risk with the Katmai and Sunspear projects. Even though as of now the projects are in good shape, to maintain current production and cash levels, they need to sustain operations. Delays or any other disruptions might cause bad results, so I think it is good to follow their disclosures and communication.

Capex is the third major risk here, to be fair. As of now it is $500 million, so a lot of cash is being reinvested into operations, and that is not giving flexibility. If the market got weaker, I believe that the company would have to cut shareholder return policies.

Conclusion

Overall, the company is good. Execution is better, projects are working, and production is stabilising. Around $400 million in free cash flow is a strong result for a company of this size. The balance is stable, and leverage seems to be controlled, so I have no doubts about their survival. For those who read my last analysis, I have intentionally left out the econometrics part, as the model did not change. There are some decoupling signs from oil in the short term, but the results are weak and not very significant to change the last model output.

My thesis still stands; I am maintaining my rating as a buy.

data.title: Bank7 Starts Off 2026 With Impressive Q1
data.description:
Earnings season for the banking industry officially kicks off this week with several large national and smaller regional banks reporting their financial results from Q1 2026. One of the companies that falls into that second category, with a market cap of just $408.6 million, is Bank7 Corporation (BSVN). If its recent press release is any indication of a wider trend, it could be a good month to be invested in the regional banking industry.

BSVN earned $1.25 per share in the first three months of this year, topping expectations by $0.23 per share, or 22.5%. That EPS total, as well as the company’s net income of $12.0 million, was a record for Bank7. As part of the press release that included a full report of the bank’s recent operations, Thomas Travis, President and CEO of Bank7, stated, “We are excited about 2026, as our properly matched balance sheet has us well positioned to continue to take advantage of our dynamic geographic region.”

For Bank7, this strong start to the year was a continuation of an impressive 2025. Many of the company’s operational metrics are well above-average. Any concerns that the economic backdrop from the start of this year, especially in the month of March, may have dragged down all members of the regional banking industry appear unwarranted. Even though BSVN has gained over 4% today on the news of its recent quarterly results, I still believe the stock is worth a Buy.

Company Overview

Based in Oklahoma City, Bank7 Corporation is the holding company for Bank7, which provides banking and other financial services to consumer and commercial clients. The company operates eight branches in its home state, two in Kansas, and two in Texas. In spite of its small size, Bank7 specializes in serving businesses in a few niche industries like energy, hospitality, and agriculture. The company claims to have a competitive advantage of fewer but “higher-quality” bankers that can accomplish more with a smaller branch footprint.

At the end of Q1 2026, BSVN reported $1.95 billion in total assets that included a loan portfolio of $1.59 billion. Both of these numbers were slight declines from Q4 2025 of less than 1%. However, when compared to Q1 2025, total assets at the bank have increased 8.9%. Total loans have risen 11.9%.

A breakdown of the loan mix for the bank is shown in the pie chart below. The largest share of loans is connected to commercial and industrial loans at $407.4 million, or 25.5%. The commitment to those niche industries mentioned earlier can be seen here. Commercial real estate allocations are split between investor CRE at 9.9%, owner-occupied CRE at 8.4%, and hospitality properties at 19.0%. Hospitality CRE would include loans to hotels, resorts, and other businesses in the hospitality industry. Bank7 also has exposure to small segments of energy and agricultural loans.

Bank7 currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.27 per share, which gives its stock a forward yield of 2.52%. The company currently has a streak of six straight years of annual payout increases. Its last dividend raise came in August of last year when it boosted its distribution by $0.03 per share. Over the last five years, the compound average growth rate of its annual dividend is just over 20%. The bank’s payout ratio of 21.6% is well below the average for other dividend-paying banks, showing that further increases in the future are likely.

Analysis of Bank7 Corporation

The most recent quarterly results for Bank7 included a number of strengths for the company that go beyond just its bottom line. However, it is important to note that the $1.25 EPS was 11.6% better than Q4 2025 and 15.7% higher than Q1 2025. The most recent earnings were produced from core operations without the benefit of any share repurchases.

Most all of the performance ratios reported by the bank are well ahead of peer banks with similar assets. Bank7’s yield on earning assets is 7.35%, supported by a loan yield of 8.03%, which is one of the highest I have seen in all of the regional banks I have written about on Seeking Alpha. The last report from the FDIC said that the average bank with assets between $1 billion and $10 billion had a loan yield of just 6.56%. Bank7's high loan yield is a big factor behind its net interest margin of 5.27%. This NIM is about 136 basis points above the FDIC-stated average.

Other strengths for the company include remarkable expense controls that have kept its efficiency ratio at or below 40% for five consecutive years. In Q1 2026, that number was just 39.64%. Return on assets in Q1 2026 was 2.56%. That number was 30 basis points better than Q4 2025 and almost twice the most recent national average.

Even with its somewhat unique and high-yielding loan portfolio, Bank7 has been able to maintain solid asset quality ratios for the last couple of years. In Q1 2026, its ratio for nonperforming assets was 0.48%, slightly higher than Q4 2025, but still well within a normal range. Nonperforming loans also increased from the previous quarter to 0.58%, but the bank was able to avoid having to report any net charge-offs in the last two quarters of results. The company actually was able to report a small recovery of about $50,000 in Q1 2026.

One other positive to report for the bank is its strong shareholder alignment with insider ownership. Insiders own approximately 55% of the company’s outstanding stock. Institutions own an additional 34%. This leaves only a little more than 10% for public ownership outside of those two groups.

Valuation Consideration

BSVN trades at a trailing PE of about 9.5, below the median for its sector. The company is expected to earn $4.36 in FY 2026, which would actually be a slight decrease from the $4.45 reported for FY 2025. However, Q1 2026 marked the 13th consecutive quarter that the company has beaten its analysts' expectations, and given the metrics discussed earlier, I see no reason to believe that trend will change in the near future.

Last year, Bank7 was able to grow its tangible book value per share to $25.26. That was a 16.4% improvement from the end of 2024 and in line with the company's performance over the last seven years. Since 2018, the compound average growth rate for BSVN's tangible book value is 16.8%.

At its current price of about $44 per share, Bank7 trades at a price-to-TBV multiple of 1.74. This is higher than the national average for all banks, which was about 1.45 at the end of FY 2025. However, the bank's profitability and strong operational metrics would seem to merit this higher valuation.

Risks to Consider

While the uniqueness of Bank7’s loan portfolio is certainly considered a strength because of the high yield it produces for the company, it could also be a weakness if the industries in which it is concentrated begin to struggle. The hospitality industry could come under pressure if consumer spending declines later this year or labor costs were to increase significantly. The energy sector, especially for oil and other natural resources, is undergoing a great deal of turmoil currently because of the conflict in the Middle East. Higher oil prices could be a boon for many companies that borrow money from Bank7, but this price spike may be relatively short-lived. The agricultural sector in many parts of the US is facing pressure from higher costs and lower market prices for their crops. In short, the exposure that Bank7 has to these different sectors bears watching in the future quarters of results.

Like a few other small regional banks that I have covered in the last year, BSVN is relatively thinly traded. There were 12 days in March when fewer than 10,000 shares of the stock traded hands. Even on April 14, when Q1 2026 earnings were released, Seeking Alpha showed a trading volume of less than 24,000 shares. This lack of activity can often cause wider spreads between the bid and the ask in the open market. Limit orders are suggested when making a purchase in Bank7 Corporation. If an investor needs to liquidate a position in a short period of time, a market sale could push the price down significantly lower than the previous transaction, depending on the size of the transaction itself.

The Bottom Line

There really is little reason for pessimism about Bank7’s prospects for the next quarter and the second half of the year. Interest rate concerns and economic headwinds may impact all banks at different times in 2026, but BSVN’s strong recent performance moves it to the front of its industry in my book. For investors who are considering additional exposure to small-cap banks, Bank7 is worth a longer look.

data.title: BlackRock Is A Buy After Q1 Despite Private Credit Exposure
data.description:
Earlier this year, I reiterated my buy rating on BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) for the second straight time, citing strong net inflows, an aggressive push into alternatives, and a valuation that was still attractive. Since then, a private credit crisis of confidence has unfolded, and a war in the Middle East has arisen. Therefore, while the stock's ~7% declines since the publication of my previous article are undesirable for the bulls, I would say that BlackRock has held up alright, all things considered. The asset management giant reported their 2026 Q1 results just this morning (April 14th), and so today, I will be providing a much-needed update for readers.

Below, it is shown that BlackRock's Q1 was another strong quarter for the firm. Net flows were overall solid, and operating margin expansion showed that the business is getting well run. BlackRock has continued its move into alternatives, and while private credit is definitely a risk worth monitoring, in the grand scheme of things their exposure is nowhere near terrifying. With the valuation below where it was in my previous article, I believe a reiteration of my buy rating is justified.

Net Flows Show Resilience

As always, a deep dive into their net flow stats should give us a good understanding of where their business is right now and the risk appetite of investors. For the firm as a whole, they saw net inflows of $130 billion. That is more than we saw in the year-ago quarter, and you can see above that the LTM organic asset growth rate has remained strong at 6%. An even more encouraging sign is the fact that LTM organic base fee growth accelerated from the previous quarter. In fact, growth has been on a steady uptrend to show that BlackRock is monetizing their huge AUM in an increasingly effective manner overall.

In my previous article, I highlighted a contrast between retail investor and institutional investor behavior, and judging from the above results, the conclusion drawn back then still applies. As shown above, for ETFs long-term, they saw net inflows of $132 billion, reflecting an LTM organic asset growth rate of 13%. This is an acceleration of growth from Q4, but on the other hand, institutional long-term saw net outflows overall. This may suggest that the 'smart money' is remaining cautious while retail investors have been a bit exuberant. Nonetheless, for BlackRock it seems that their iShares business is booming, and of course that is a great sign for the company as a whole.

Core Profitability Looking Good

In their Q1, BlackRock generated total revenues of $6.698 billion, up 27% YoY. This is a notable speedup in top line growth from Q4's 23% growth rate, and so business activity is heating up for the firm. Furthermore, they also beat analyst expectations by around $260 million, and so Q1 was a quarter of outperformance. In addition to the top line numbers, profitability also fared quite well.

For adjusted EPS, BlackRock saw an 11% increase, which is an acceleration from the previous quarter as well. While the bottom line grew slower than the top line, they were still able to surpass estimates by $1.03. In addition, operating margin is the better reflection of where the core profitability of the firm is at. In adjusted terms, operating margin expanded considerably from the prior year period's 43.2% to 44.5% to show that BlackRock is well run. Business efficiency continues to improve, and really, an operating margin in the 40s percentage range is very healthy.

Shareholders Enjoy Nice Capital Returns

With BlackRock's business booming, strong capital returns mean that shareholders get their share of the wealth. For their dividend, as previously promised, they have upped it to $5.73 per share, and as of now their yield is at an actually quite respectable 2.24%. In addition, after authorizing an additional 7 million shares for repurchase back in Q4, BlackRock upped their share repurchases YoY from $375 million to $450 million. These actions are obviously welcomed by investors, and in my view, they also demonstrate the continued confidence by the management team regarding their outlook.

Alternatives Push Continues

As stated in the introduction, BlackRock is pushing aggressively into alternatives. In their Q1, clients assets increased by 50% to $687 billion, while fee-paying AUM also increased by a very respectable 39% to $531 billion. These growth rates are nearly unchanged from the previous quarter, and so it is clear that the firm is continuing to push into this area. Alternatives have been historically a very lucrative space, and so their continued ambitions here could pay off in the long run.

At the end of March, it was reported that the Trump Administration is proposing to include alternatives in 401(k) plans. This could result in a significant reallocation of capital. While this could take away from some of BlackRock's other businesses, it could provide major tailwinds for their alternative efforts. If these new rules ultimately do get implemented, shareholders will look back on BlackRock's move to pursue this area as a key strategic decision.

Private Credit Risks

There is little question that the largest risk facing BlackRock today is their notable exposure to private credit. If you go and take a look back at the alternatives chart above, you can see that as of the end of Q1, they have $210 billion of client assets and $147 billion of fee-paying AUM in private credit. Therefore, if the crisis of confidence continues, BlackRock may see some effects.

Let's go over a timeline of how private credit risks have emerged at BlackRock. In early March, the company wrote down a second private loan to zero. To amplify the concerns, just a few months ago this loan was valued at 100 cents on the dollar. Such a rapid collapse in value shows that the private credit space likely has issues with mark-to-market policies. Just a day after this news broke out, BlackRock had to limit withdrawals from their private credit fund as a sign that there's a major lack of confidence. Despite these concerning signs, in reference to Mohamed El-Erian's comments about parallels to 2007, CEO Larry Fink stated that

I would love to debate Mohamed right now. I don’t see any similarities at all. Zero. 2007 was based on hidden leverage, just gigantic leverage balance sheets. This is not a leverage balance sheet problem.

Fink's views are also a stark contrast to comments that JPMorgan (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon made just quite recently, and so at this point, we really do not know how this crisis will play out. For BlackRock, this is nonetheless a notable risk that they have to deal with. Lastly, earlier this month, it was reported that lawmakers are probing private credit firms, including BlackRock, with regard to their private credit practices. This is a suggestion that actions may have been questionable.

Valuation Analysis

At the current reading of 20.64, the forward P/E ratio for BlackRock is below levels seen back in my previous update. With the valuation far below the highs seen last year, I believe the risk/reward remains net attractive for investors despite the private credit saga that's still playing out.

Today, it was shown that BlackRock is continuing to experience robust inflows as a sign that business activity has remained high. With LTM organic fee growth accelerating, their monetization of this activity is also improving. In terms of profitability, an expanding operating margin clearly shows that BlackRock is being run in a very efficient manner. Finally, their continued alternatives push is justified, especially given the Trump Administration's 401(k) proposal.

While the company does have a notable exposure to private credit, it should also be mentioned that their exposure isn't at terrifying levels. Relative to the entire firm's $13.89 trillion AUM at the end of Q1, private credit is a business that's unlikely to cause any kind of existential crisis for BlackRock, and so despite the concerning signs in that space, I believe the firm is still overall on solid footing.

With that in mind, I would say that the slight valuation contraction since my previous article presents investors with a chance to buy into this asset management giant. The private credit crisis and Iran war have seemingly kept a lid on the stock price, but given the current fundamental setup, I believe stock outperformance is likely moving forward.

Conclusion

BlackRock has experienced mild losses since my previous article, and while that was unexpected, I believe the stock is ready for outperformance after reporting solid Q1 numbers. As a whole, I remain impressed with their fundamental strength, especially given the macro and geopolitical environment. While private credit is indeed a risk worth monitoring, it is also a risk that has seemingly been somewhat overblown. While headlines of write downs and withdrawal limits have spooked some investors, their exposure to this space is not overly large considering their total AUM. With the valuation below where it was back in my previous update, I believe the risk/reward remains attractive enough to justify another reiteration of the buy rating.

data.title: Towering 10-Day A/D Lines
data.description:
The Iran war resulted in a tough March for US equities. In addition to the S&P 500 falling close to 10% versus its 52-week high, breadth took a measurable hit. As shown below, up until the first week of March, the 10-day advance/decline line, which measures the net share of index members rising or falling each day over ten days, was positive for most of the year.

Once equities began selling off, this measure tanked, and at the low on March 13th, it reached the lowest level since 12/19/24, which also ranks as a 2nd percentile reading going back to 1990.

With the benefit of hindsight, we can now say that low in breadth pre-dated the low in price (which came on March 30th). It has now been ten trading days since the low, and daily breadth has been positive all but twice (April 7th and April 10th were the only days with negative daily breadth). As a result, the 10-day A/D line is now at the highest levels since 7/10/25.

As we highlighted in yesterday's Sector Snapshot, not only has the S&P 500's 10-day A/D line risen sharply, but several other sectors have as well. As shown below, Consumer Discretionary, Industrials, and Real Estate all went from extreme oversold readings (2+ standard deviations below the historical average) to extreme overbought readings today. While the reading isn't quite as extreme, Tech has also seen a sharp rise in its line.

As might be evident from the one-year charts above, by far the most extended breadth measure comes out of the real estate sector. To give greater historical context, below we show the 10-day A/D line going back to September 2016, when it became a sector.

With another move higher today, real estate's 10-day A/D line is at the highest level since 2/2/23. The only other times the 10-day A/D line was more elevated were from January to February 2019 and in June 2020.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Closer Look At Full-Time And Part-Time Employment: March 2026
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Buried near the bottom of table A-9 of the government's employment situation summary are the numbers for full- and part-time workers, with 35-or-more hours as the arbitrary divide between the two categories. The source is the monthly current population survey (CPS) of households. The focus is on total hours worked regardless of whether the hours are from a single or multiple jobs.

The Labor Department has been collecting this since 1968, a time when only 13.5% of US employees were part-timers. Over 55 years later, March's employment report showed that 17.4% of total employed workers were part time and 82.6% of total employed workers were full-time.

Here is a visualization of the trend in the 21st century, with the percentage of full-time employed on the left axis and the part-time employed on the right. We see a noticeable crossover during the Great Recession when part-time work increased, and full-time work declined. Over the past few years, we have seen a similar crossover slowly form.

The Impact of Recessions on Employment

Let's zoom in a bit closer to see the impact of the Great Recession on employment. We’ve changed the start date of our next chart to the beginning of 2007. The reversal began in 2008, but it accelerated in the fall of that year following the September 15th bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers.

In January 2010, shortly after the official end of the recession, part-time work peaked at 20.1%, while full-time work reached an all-time low of 79.9%. Afterwards, the two cohorts slowly drifted apart, with full-time employment increasing.

Interestingly, this trend continued even during the COVID global pandemic and recession. It was only until recently that we started to see a slight reversal in this trend, with full-time work slowly declining while part-time work is on the rise. In December 2023, full-time employment dropped below 83.0% of all employment while part-time employment rose above 17.0% and we have been there ever since.

Here's a longer-term view of this same chart. With the extended timeline, we can see a few more examples of what has typically happened to these two kinds of work during a recession.

You’ll notice that the recessions from the early 90s and early 2000s saw similar action as the Great Recession: Full-time work declined while part-time work rose. But again, this was not the case during the most recent recession in 2020.

In fact, full-time employment peaked in April 2020, possibly because of a shift in the type of full-time work available at the start of the pandemic. There were some employees who shifted to part-time from full-time as a result of the pandemic; however, it was only about 3% of those working part-time.

One more interesting feature in the chart above is the dramatic change in the full-time/part-time ratio at the beginning of 1994. In January 1994, the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) came into effect. This was a trade agreement between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, aimed at reducing barriers to trade and investment among the three countries.

While NAFTA led to increased trade and economic growth overall, it also had various effects on employment. Some industries saw an increase in part-time work due to outsourcing or changes in demand, while others experienced a decline in full-time positions.

Let's have a closer look at why some employees work part-time, voluntarily and involuntarily:

A noteworthy trend we have seen over the past few years is the rise in part-time work for involuntary reasons.

A Closer Look at the Core Workforce, Ages 25-54

The two charts above are seasonally adjusted and include the entire workforce, which the CPS defines as age 16 and over. A problem inherent in using this broadest of cohorts is that it includes the population that adds substantial summertime volatility to the full-time/part-time ratio, namely, high school and college students.

Also, the 55-plus cohort includes a subset of employees that opt for part-time employment during the decade following the historical peak earning years (ages 45-54) and as a transition toward retirement.

The next chart better illustrates summertime volatility by focusing on the change since 2007 in full- and part-time employment for the 25-54 workforce. The government's full-time/part-time data for this cohort is only available as non-seasonally adjusted. To help us recognize the summer seasonality, the June-July-August markers are shown in a lighter color.

These months are more subject to temporary shifts from part-time to 35-plus hours of employment. The 12-month moving averages for the two series help us identify the slope of the trend in recent years. Again, we see that over the past few months the trend has reversed where part-time employment has increased while full-time employment has decreased.

In March 2026, part-time employment for the "core" workforce made up 11.3% of all "core" employment.

Like the 16-and-over version, this chart depicts a current situation that is considerably different from the era before the financial crisis, although the trend has been slowly improving since early 2010, as illustrated by the dotted-line moving averages. In this non-seasonally adjusted data, the summer months are obvious outliers, with full-time employment spiking during the June-August period.

Obamacare and Part-Time Employment

Many have speculated that the Affordable Care Act (aka Obamacare) has played a role in company decisions about full-time versus part-time employment. The $2,000 per employee penalty for companies that do not comply with regulations has influenced some to rethink their employment policies.

In July 2013, the government pushed the start of the penalty from January 2014 to January 2015. But the anticipation of the penalty, even though delayed a year, no doubt influenced the decisions of private employers.

With regard to Obamacare and part-time employment, the surge in part-time employment was triggered by the recession, not by the Affordable Care Act, as the next chart clearly illustrates.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics' monthly employment report is a hodgepodge of data. The full-time/part-time ratio is but a tiny piece of the whole, and the magic 35-hour threshold is arguably arbitrary. But this is, nevertheless, a metric that bears close watching as we try to understand where the economy is headed.

The question is whether the ratio shift is to some extent a structural change initially triggered by the great recession but now influenced by a combination of factors, not least of which are workplace demographics and ongoing increases in technology-driven productivity with less dependence on human workforce participation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: XSD In The Semiconductor Boom, But Not The Choice I Prefer. Here's Why
data.description:
I am very fascinated by SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) because it positions itself in a highly vertical market concentrated on a few stocks with an unusual approach: equal weight. An approach that brings added value to the portfolio? Although ideologically it seems like a real “breath of fresh air” to reduce excessive weights, such as those of names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), my honest opinion is the opposite. In my view, doing so would distort the semiconductor market. A market that today, in my opinion, deserves particular attention, despite the conflict in Iran. But let me explain better…

What is XSD?

XSD is in all respects a pure sector index instrument on semiconductors, with systematic and optimized replication of the benchmark, the S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index (a widely used index built with a modified equal-weighted methodology).

The expense ratio mentioned multiple times is 0.35%, barely covered by the dividend yield, which TTM is equal to 0.21% (which is natural considering it represents the quintessential US growth segment).

How is It Built?

The fund is naturally 100% exposed to semiconductors, with a focus on the US segment, and the most interesting and distinctive aspect is precisely that the benchmark is modified equal-weighted.

This introduces a clear economic effect, namely the strong reduction in the weight of mega-caps and the increase in the relative weight of mid- and small-caps. Not surprisingly, there are 45 holdings, and the top 10 account for less than 30% (unusual for this type of sector ETF).

It is interesting how, although it is still true that no single stock really dominates, the choice not to give weight to the most capitalized semiconductor companies, in my opinion, still leads to an imbalance. To be clear, in XSD tickers like Nvidia, which practically control the market, almost “disappear” from the radar, ceasing to be the dominant driver of sector performance. Good, you might think… yet the truth is that today, they are dominant drivers of the sector.

I think the fund can offer exposure to emerging or adjacent segments but also introduce complexity and volatility that are less immediately readable. And this is an element that clearly emerges when we examine the daily holding distribution shared by SPDR. In fact, about 78.8% of the weight in XSD is skewed toward segments below the large-cap category; those stocks that populate market-weight ETFs (names below $10B control 78.8% of XSD). Only 21% is covered by Nvidia, Broadcom, Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD), QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM), Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN), Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), and Intel Corporation (INTC).

Peer Analysis

And peer analysis becomes central in this context, and personally I think the comparison with the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), two market-weighted ETFs, is very interesting. These ETFs are much more concentrated in mega-leaders. Here is a comparison from SA data useful to understand the differences in the holding distribution.

And at this point, it is interesting to note how, by extending the time frame, market-weighted solutions have outperformed with the arrival of AI.

And the fundamental reason is only one: AI is extremely capital-intensive, and hyperscalers’ CapEx has gone directly to mega-caps.

Big Tech has announced an increase in CAPEX. The money goes to the big players in the semiconductor sector; in the AI ecosystem, there are always Nvidia, Broadcom, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), and Advanced Micro Devices. It does not go to Cirrus Logic, Inc. (CRUS) or SiTime Corporation (SITM). Why is that? Simply because in semiconductors the winning software architecture creates hardware lock-in. Nvidia’s CUDA is not just a GPU; it is an ecosystem of 15 years of libraries, tools, and accustomed developers. It is no coincidence that Nvidia has around 80% of the high-end AI data center chip market, with estimates above 90% in training workloads.

Let’s Narrow the Horizon From March

By narrowing the time horizon to 1M, we notice how, compared to the lows reached at the end of March, this segment is recovering across all 3 ETFs.

And this is normal; with the conflict in Iran, valuations, especially in the tech sector, have returned to the 10- and 30-year averages. But it is also true that Information Technology is the main driver of earnings growth for the S&P 500 in Q1 2026, and within IT the dominant contribution comes precisely from “Semiconductors & Semiconductor Equipment.” More specifically, FactSet writes that IT is expected to be at +45.0% YoY in earnings, the highest figure among all sectors, and that the Semiconductors & Semiconductor Equipment industry is the main contributor; without this group, IT earnings growth would drop to 20.1% from 45.0%. This comes in response to a net margin for Information Technology expected at 28.9% vs. 25.4% a year ago. Considering that CapEx has increased by 70% YoY, this would be a very important confirmation for this segment. And with the evolution of the conflict and Trump’s rhetoric shifting from truce to new blockades of the Strait of Hormuz, the market has started to understand Trump’s trade strategy and assign less weight to it on forward valuations (which had become very cheap).

My Opinion

And according to data updated as of April 10, they still are: the 12-month forward P/E of tech is at 22x, below the 5-year average and around the 10-year average.

If confirmed, this would make the upward CAPEX path sustainable, which would positively impact semiconductor guidance. Therefore, I am bullish in this direction on this segment.

Risk

However… specifically, I am not very bullish on XSD. This is precisely because the semiconductor sector seems to be increasingly taking on a vertical dimension toward a few players with distinctive MOATs. And an ETF that instead assigns weights diverging from those given by the market, in my opinion, will not bring added value. Considering that the contribution is not there statistically, even in terms of standard deviation and annualized volatility compared to other ETFs, I would prefer to use cap-weighted ETFs as a reference.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on this segment, but I think the right rating for XSD is HOLD. This is because, in my opinion, the equal weight formula poorly represents the (current) underlying structure of this market, and I do not see positive catalysts in this sense. That said, I remain bullish on the semiconductor segment: the market is assigning less weight to the impact of Trump’s geopolitical choices and has chosen to buy forward valuations at the 10-year average
